The importance of success in your own life and how you value money, like how do you look at money? Do you look at money in a healthy way? Would you let money control you, make you miserable? So here's the thing, I just, you know, guys, I've always been a big owner, right? Since I was 16 years old, walking on the beach, selling ice as I was making more money than my parents, my parents, friends. It's making, you know, $500, $600 a day back in the 80s, well, no, the 70s, 1978 guys, which is the equivalent of probably 1500, 2000 bucks a day. And I was 16 years old. And it changed my perspective on wealth and success, my own ability to achieve it. So I had always been an entrepreneur growing up, and this was, it was a breakaway experience for me. It just, it changed my perspective. And I realized in that moment that the harder I work, the more money I make. And also the more money I make, and you know, for better or worse, people have more respect for me. Seriously, I remember back when I was in college and I had, you know, my first real girlfriend, right? She was very wealthy. And her father was a super rich guy, and I was from a poor family. And I went to their house for the first time up in Westchester, New York, and it was Ronnie Russell, great girl. We still keep in touch from time to time, right? Wonderful girl, very beautiful, right? And here's the deal. When I walked in that house, I, I, I, it's like entering the Emerald Castle in the Wizard of Oz. I couldn't believe it. They had a pool in their house. They just saw some crazy furniture. It was massive. I couldn't believe it. It was up in Harrison, New York, right? And her father instantly took a liking to me when he found out that I was hustling on the beach, making money. Then when I opened up a mouth to speak, he, he says that I had a bit of a gift, right? Or a lot of a gift, right? Anyway, we went to this party with all her father's rich friends and their parents got divorced. So he moved to Ft.L. New Jersey, went to a party in his penthouse in Ft.L. And he introduced me to his friends and he said, guys, remember this kid. He's going to be richer than all of us. This kid's going to be a force to be reckoned with. He said to me, I was, I was 19 years old at the time and he said that to his friends who were all rich. You know how that made me feel inside? And it made me feel like, I mean, honestly, guys, it made, it made all the hard work worth it to me at that point. I felt like I had already arrived. Even though I was still a kid. And then he flipped me the keys to his Mercedes 450 SL convertible and his door right to the right to Atlantic City. I mean, he was a great guy, right? May rest in peace. Here's my point. You need to be this person. I want you to be the person that when people meet you, when people speak to you, when people hear your story like, that person, that guy, that girl, that, that's a force to be reckoned with. That person's special. They're going to stick out. Now, what did he see in me? And what should people see in you? Number one, a bubble, massive hard work. Right? I was out there hustling. He says, this guy gets up at five o'clock in the morning, goes down to the distributor. He's like his mother buttering bagels for him. There's another mother working for me. He's got four kids carrying jewelry. He's still a number one hard work. Number two, entrepreneurial spirit. Number three, a total lack of fear of any rejection of, you know, of not succeeding. Just didn't enter my vernacular. To me, I was, you know, I was going in one way and that was straight to the top. And he saw that in me and he commented. And my point to you is that you can be that person too. You can be that ball of light, that person. Now, when you walk in a room that we meet people that like, bam, that's not. That's an extraordinary human being. Now, how do you get there? How do you do that? Well, a big part of it is mastering the art of communication. See, there's because he has the deal, right? Bill Gates could have walked into the same room as me at the same time. And I'm sure that his father would not have said, oh, this guy destined for greatness. And by the way, look what happened with Bill Gates, right? He became far richer than I ever, ever did and probably ever will. I'm sure, right? But the point is, maybe not. We'll see. Anyway, the point is, is that it's the ability to communicate what you have to offer the world. What's inside you? There's so many people out there who have amazing gifts and talents and abilities and ideas and visions for their future that will not just impact them, but their families and they love their communities. But because they can't communicate that vision to other people, they're blocked from achieving it. It's locked in sudden. You know, the expression uses a dye with their music on their lips. They can't get it out. The true beauty really of the straight line besides that, yes, you can close anyone is close. That we all know. Okay. You've seen that your experience yourself beyond that. It's the ability to make yourself known to the world. Whether you're online doing a video that you're taping and you want to have people come to your landing page, whether you walk in a room in a cocktail party and you're so in that when you open up your mouth, people hang on your every word. You got to tell a story. You're going to be a 60 second elevator pitch. In fact, one of the topics next year was also a quick elevator pitch. You know, 60 second pitches. One of the things I'm really, really, you have a great program for it. Point being here. I'm heading to the airport because we're in a tunnel now. The point being guys is that, yes, you can have the greatest idea in the world, but you have to be able to communicate that idea in a way that connects with other human beings and it powers them. Makes them want to take action to help you achieve your dream, get you what you want to. That's ethical persuasion. When I was in Africa, I did a training for a bunch of really impoverished kids in South Africa. I went to one of the two. It was actually Nelson Mandela's talent show where he grew up. And these are all the kids. They call them Eagles. They were the leaders of the community. Right. I think it's my dream here. Right. It's a great story. So, and by the way, you can actually see this training online. You've all, I have a lot of people posted in the back end. Yes, you can look at it. It's called Jordan Belpin, my Africa Ticlin. And I did a series of trainings for these kids where I traveled to Africa, went into Zambia, went into South Africa. I worked with these kids. I created a program for them. And I said to them, I tried to say, you know, how do I serve these kids? You know, what can I say to them that makes this real for them? Because I, you know, I can't talk about business at a high level there. They're living in poverty. Right. So what I basically said to them is this, and there's three things that you must have in your life to be successful, to be an asset to your community, to rise up above where you are right now. There's three things that you have to possess. Number one, I didn't say sharp as a tack. I didn't say that. Number one, you must have a vision for your future. You have to have the vision. I want to explain to them that people don't follow you because of a goal. People follow vision. Every human being starts to defer vision. Very few have one. So what do people do? They follow someone who has a compelling vision. That's a fact. That's why there's leaders and this follows. So you must have that ultra compelling and sparring vision for the future. Number two, you have to have the ability to share that vision. You get it to sell that vision to other people. Again, if you have this great vision, this great idea, this great plan in mind, but it's locked inside of you, can't get it out. You'll die with your music. And that's sales, my friend. That's why you're in this program. You're like, listen, you have this cover now. And if you're not there yet, you'll be there soon. You'll get better and better and better about conflict, never ending improvement. Right? So that second aspect, you know, you have the vision. Yeah. Now you got to share that vision in a way that inspires people and wants to jump on board to essentially buy into your vision with their time, their money, their sweat, equity, their elbow grease, so to speak, and turn your vision into a reality.
